stem: A hospital uses a blood screening test to screen for a rare genetic condition. The test has a sensitivity of 80% and a specificity of 80%. The prevalence of a rare genetic condition in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.1% and 3.6%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has a rare genetic condition? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(a rare genetic condition | positive test) ranges from 0.079 to 0.13 as prevalence varies from 2.1% to 3.6%. Point estimate at 2.8% prevalence: 0.103.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.2.
The confidence interval [0.079, 0.13] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (80%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
